stem: What is the default maximum speed for a car inside a built-up area where no signs say otherwise?

explanation.short: Inside a built-up area, the default maximum for cars is 50 km/h.
explanation.examTip: Many residential and shopping streets are signed at 30 km/h, which overrides the default.

stem: Outside a built-up area on a normal road, what is the default maximum speed for a car?

explanation.short: On normal roads outside a built-up area, the default for cars is 80 km/h.
explanation.examTip: Expressways (autoweg) raise this to 100 km/h; motorways higher still where signed.

stem: On a Dutch motorway with no signed exception, what is the maximum speed for a car between 06:00 and 19:00?

explanation.short: Between 06:00 and 19:00, cars are capped at 100 km/h on motorways unless a sign says otherwise.
explanation.examTip: Outside daytime hours, the limit follows the signed value, which is often 120 or 130 km/h.

stem: In a woonerf (residential yard zone), what speed must you drive at?

explanation.short: In a woonerf you must drive at walking pace, roughly 15 km/h, and yield to pedestrians using the whole street.
explanation.examTip: Pedestrians, and especially playing children, have priority over vehicles in a woonerf.

stem: If you double your speed, what happens to the reaction distance (assuming the same reaction time)?

explanation.short: Reaction distance grows in proportion to speed, so doubling speed roughly doubles it.
explanation.examTip: Reaction distance is fixed by your reaction time; only braking distance grows with the square of speed.

image.alt: Bar comparison showing reaction distance at 100 km/h is roughly double that at 50 km/h.
image.caption: Reaction distance grows in proportion to speed

stem: If you double your speed, what happens to the braking distance on the same road and tyres?

explanation.short: Braking distance grows with the square of speed, so doubling speed roughly quadruples it.
explanation.examTip: This is why a small increase from 50 to 60 km/h adds far more stopping distance than it looks.

stem: Total stopping distance is best described as:

explanation.short: Total stopping distance is the sum of how far you travel while reacting and how far you travel while braking.
explanation.examTip: Both components grow with speed, but braking grows much faster — so high speeds dominate the total.

stem: Compared to a dry road, what is the most realistic effect of a wet road on braking distance?

explanation.short: A wet road can roughly double braking distance compared to dry tarmac.
explanation.examTip: Worn tyres make the effect worse — reduce speed in advance rather than relying on hard braking.

stem: In dense fog, how should you choose your speed?

choices.text: Drive at the signed limit because it is the legal speed
Drive slowly enough to stop within the distance you can see to be clear
Drive faster to leave the fog quickly
Drive at half the signed limit regardless

explanation.short: Your speed must always let you stop within the distance you can see to be clear, even well below the signed limit.
explanation.examTip: Speed limits are upper bounds — conditions can require a much lower speed.

stem: In dry conditions, what is a common rule of thumb for safe following distance behind the car in front?

choices.text: Close enough to read the number plate

explanation.short: Roughly a two-second gap is the standard guideline in dry conditions.
explanation.examTip: Double the gap on wet roads, and extend it further in poor visibility or behind heavy vehicles.

stem: What is the standard daytime (06:00–19:00) speed limit on Dutch motorways for cars?

explanation.short: Since 2020 the default daytime motorway limit is 100 km/h; higher limits apply only where signed and only at night.
explanation.examTip: Always obey the signed limit — it overrides the default in either direction.

stem: You see the rectangular blue sign that marks a built-up area. From that point the default speed limit is:

explanation.short: Inside a built-up area the default limit is 50 km/h unless lower limits (30 km/h zones) are signed.
explanation.examTip: Leaving the area is shown by the same sign with a red diagonal bar — default rises to 80 km/h.

stem: Stopping distance is made up of which two parts?

explanation.short: Total stopping distance = reaction distance (before the brakes act) + braking distance (while the brakes act).
explanation.examTip: Fatigue, alcohol and phone use mainly stretch the reaction part, not the brake part.

image.alt: A bar split into two coloured segments — reaction distance and braking distance — which together make up the total stopping distance.
image.caption: Stopping distance = reaction distance + braking distance

stem: If you double your speed, the braking distance on dry tarmac becomes roughly:

explanation.short: Braking distance grows with the square of speed, so doubling speed roughly quadruples it.
explanation.examTip: Reaction distance only doubles — but the squared effect on braking is what makes high-speed crashes severe.

image.alt: A bar chart comparing braking distance: a short bar for 50 km/h and a bar roughly four times longer for 100 km/h.
image.caption: Double the speed, roughly four times the braking distance

stem: It is raining and the road surface is wet. How should you adjust your following distance compared to dry conditions?

choices.text: Reduce it slightly to keep up

explanation.short: On a wet road grip drops, so roughly double the two-second guideline to about four seconds.
explanation.examTip: On ice or hard-packed snow, an even larger gap (eight to ten seconds) is reasonable.

stem: Another driver is tailgating you on a single-lane road. What is the safest response?

choices.text: Brake hard to make them back off
Keep your speed and increase the gap to the car in front
Speed up well above the limit
Wave them past while turning around

explanation.short: Hold your line, leave more space ahead, and let them overtake when it is safe.
explanation.examTip: Brake-checking a tailgater is dangerous and may be treated as reckless driving.

stem: At 14:00 on a clear, dry day you drive on a Dutch motorway where the signed maximum is 130 km/h. What is your legal maximum speed?

choices.text: 130 km/h, because that is the signed maximum
100 km/h, because of the daytime cap between 06:00 and 19:00

explanation.short: Between 06:00 and 19:00 cars are capped at 100 km/h regardless of higher signed limits.
explanation.examTip: The 130 km/h sign only applies at night — 100 km/h is the legal max during the day.

stem: On a dry road at 50 km/h your braking distance is about 12 m. You drive the same route at the same speed in heavy rain. Roughly what is the braking distance now?

choices.text: About 12 m — speed has not changed
About 24 m — wet roads roughly double braking distance
About 6 m — water lubricates the brakes
About 50 m — wet roads quadruple braking distance

explanation.short: Wet roads roughly double the braking distance; reaction distance is unchanged.
explanation.examTip: Doubling speed quadruples braking; wet roads double the braking part again.

stem: It has just started raining after a dry week. You are following a lorry at 80 km/h with a 2-second gap. What should you do?

choices.text: Keep the 2-second gap — it is correct for any speed
Drop back to at least 4 seconds because of wet road and lorry's blocked view
Move closer to use the lorry as a windbreak
Overtake immediately — the first rain is always brief

explanation.short: First-rain conditions roughly double braking distance; behind a heavy vehicle, leave even more gap.
explanation.examTip: Wet: double the gap to 4 seconds; behind a lorry, add more because their bulk blocks your view ahead.

stem: You are driving in fog. Visibility drops to about 40 m. Which lights may you legally use?

choices.text: Main beam only — to cut through the fog
Dipped headlights and front fog lights only
Dipped headlights, front fog lights, and rear fog lights

explanation.short: Front fog lights below 200 m visibility; rear fog lights only below 50 m. At 40 m visibility both are allowed.
explanation.examTip: Switch rear fog lights off as soon as visibility improves — they dazzle the driver behind you.

stem: You are following another car on a wet provincial road at 80 km/h. Normally you would keep a two-second gap. What is the safer following distance now?

choices.text: One second — you are paying attention
At least four seconds, because braking distance roughly doubles on wet roads

explanation.short: On a wet surface braking distance roughly doubles, so the two-second rule should also double to about four seconds.
explanation.examTip: Use a fixed point ahead and count 'twenty-one, twenty-two, twenty-three, twenty-four' after the car ahead passes it.

stem: You enter a residential street under a sign showing 'zone 30'. There are no further speed signs after the sign. What is the maximum speed and how should you adjust your driving?

choices.text: 30 km/h until an 'end of zone' sign, and you should expect children and play
30 km/h only at the sign itself, then back to 50 km/h
No limit unless a round red-and-white sign is posted

explanation.short: A 'zone 30' sign sets 30 km/h for the entire zone until the cancellation sign; design and behaviour should match that limit.
explanation.examTip: Do not confuse a 30 km/h zone with a woonerf — in a woonerf the limit is walking pace (about 15 km/h).

stem: Travelling at 50 km/h with a 1-second reaction time, approximately how far does your car travel before you even begin to brake?

explanation.short: At 50 km/h (roughly 14 m/s) you travel about 14 metres in 1 second before your foot reaches the brake.
explanation.examTip: Quick formula: speed in km/h divided by 3.6 gives metres per second. At 50 km/h: 50 ÷ 3.6 ≈ 14 m/s.

stem: If your braking distance at 50 km/h is 14 metres on a dry road, approximately what is it at 100 km/h on the same road?

choices.text: About 28 metres — braking distance doubles with speed
About 42 metres — braking distance triples with speed
About 56 metres — braking distance quadruples when speed doubles
About 70 metres — braking distance quintuples with speed

explanation.short: Braking distance increases with the square of speed. Doubling speed from 50 to 100 km/h means 2² = 4 times the braking distance: 14 × 4 = 56 metres.
explanation.examTip: Speed doubled → braking distance quadrupled. This is one of the most important physics principles on the CBR exam.

image.alt: Bar comparison showing braking distance grows from 14 metres at 50 km/h to 56 metres at 100 km/h.
image.caption: Double the speed means four times the braking distance

stem: You drive into a street marked with a 'woonerf' (shared residential space) sign. What is the maximum permitted speed?

explanation.short: In a woonerf (shared play-street), the maximum speed is 15 km/h — walking pace — because pedestrians, children, and play are expected on the road surface.
explanation.examTip: Woonerf = 15 km/h. Do not confuse with a 30 km/h zone, which is an ordinary residential street, not a shared space.

stem: On a Dutch motorway with a permanent posted limit of 130 km/h, what is the maximum speed between 19:00 and 06:00?

choices.text: 100 km/h — the daytime restriction still applies at night
120 km/h — all motorways are capped at 120 at night
130 km/h — the posted sign applies outside the daytime restriction hours
Unlimited — there is no national cap after 19:00

explanation.short: The 100 km/h restriction runs only from 06:00 to 19:00. Outside those hours the permanent signed limit applies — so 130 km/h on a 130-posted motorway.
explanation.examTip: The 100 km/h rule is a time-of-day restriction, not the road's permanent limit. At night the road reverts to its posted sign.

stem: On a motorway at 100 km/h in dry conditions, what is the recommended minimum following distance behind the vehicle in front?

choices.text: At least 10 metres regardless of speed

explanation.short: The 2-second rule gives you enough time to react and brake if the car ahead stops suddenly. Increase to 3 or more seconds in poor conditions or at higher speed.
explanation.examTip: To check your gap: pick a fixed point. When the car ahead passes it, say 'one thousand and one, one thousand and two.' If you reach the point before finishing, you are too close.

stem: Fog reduces your forward visibility to 40 metres. What is the key speed principle you must apply?

choices.text: Use the posted speed limit — fog lights compensate for reduced visibility
Drive at 50 km/h regardless of road type
Drive at a speed that allows you to stop safely within your range of vision
Reduce your speed by exactly half the posted limit

explanation.short: The rule is simple: if you cannot stop within the distance you can see, you are driving too fast. With 40 m visibility, your total stopping distance must stay under 40 m.
explanation.examTip: At 50 km/h the total stopping distance in good conditions is about 28 m. In thick fog, add wet-road braking and the 40 m sight line — you should be well under 50 km/h.

stem: You see a speed sign with a yellow background showing '70'. What does this mean?

choices.text: The legal maximum speed is 70 km/h — you must not exceed it
This is an advisory speed for the conditions ahead — a recommendation, not a legal limit
This is the minimum speed — you must drive at least 70 km/h
This is a zone limit applying to both directions of the road

explanation.short: A yellow-background speed sign is an advisory (aanbevolen snelheid), not a legal maximum. It suggests the conditions ahead — such as a sharp bend — call for that speed or less.
explanation.examTip: White or black background = legal maximum. Yellow background = advice only. You will not be fined for exceeding it, but ignoring it is a safety risk.

stem: You are towing a caravan on a Dutch motorway with a posted limit of 130 km/h. What is your maximum legal speed?

choices.text: 130 km/h — the posted limit applies to all vehicles
100 km/h — the daytime restriction applies to towing vehicles too
80 km/h — trailers cap all road types at 80 km/h
90 km/h — the limit for car-and-caravan combinations on motorways

explanation.short: A car towing a caravan or trailer on Dutch motorways is limited to 90 km/h, regardless of the posted sign or the daytime 100 km/h restriction.
explanation.examTip: Car + caravan/trailer = 90 km/h maximum on a motorway. This applies day and night.

stem: You are driving at 80 km/h on a wet road. How does your total stopping distance compare to the same speed on a dry road?

choices.text: It is the same — ABS eliminates the difference between wet and dry
It is roughly the same — rain affects visibility but not braking
It is significantly longer — wet roads reduce tyre grip and increase braking distance
It is shorter — water cools the tyres and improves grip

explanation.short: Wet surfaces significantly reduce tyre-road friction, meaning braking distance can be 50% or more longer than on a dry road at the same speed.
explanation.examTip: ABS helps prevent wheel lock-up but does not restore grip lost on wet surfaces. Always increase following distance in rain.

stem: A child runs onto the road 30 metres ahead and you react immediately. At what approximate maximum speed could you stop in time on a dry road?

explanation.short: At 50 km/h, the total stopping distance (reaction + braking) is approximately 28–30 metres on a dry road. Any faster and 30 metres is not enough.
explanation.examTip: This is why 30 km/h zones exist near schools — at 30 km/h total stopping distance is around 12–14 m, giving far more margin for unexpected events.
